record_office_terminology.probate: Probate Court (formerly Court of Ordinary, renamed 1868)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Probate Court (county-level registration from formation; statewide index 1952)

burned_county_authority: Paul K. Graham, 'Courthouse Disasters in Georgia' (Georgia Genealogical Society Quarterly); FamilySearch Research Wiki — Georgia Burned Counties; Georgia Archives county records guides; Ted O. Brooke, 'In the Land of the Living: Warrants and Surveys of Colonial Georgia, 1755-1775'
administrative_notes: Georgia was one of the 13 original colonies (founded 1733). It has 159 counties — second most in the US after Texas. Colonial records (1733-1777) used a parish system; the 1777 constitution created 8 original counties from parishes. Georgia's land distribution used a unique lottery system (1805-1833) rather than the federal land office system. The state capital moved: Savannah (1733-1786), Augusta (1786-1795), Louisville (1796-1807), Milledgeville (1807-1868), Atlanta (1868-present). Two counties were abolished: Campbell (1932, merged to Fulton) and Milton (1932, merged to Fulton) — their records are in Fulton County.

core_repositories.description: Primary state repository. Houses microfilmed county records (deeds, marriages, wills, tax digests), colonial records, state government records, military records, and vital records. Virtual Vault provides online access to some digitized collections. Tax digests (1790-1900+) are essential — often the only surviving records for burned counties.
Digitized records including vanishing Georgia photographs, Civil War records, colonial records, and selected county records. Searchable name index for some collections.

Oldest cultural institution in Georgia (founded 1839). Manuscript collections, photographs, maps, and published genealogies. Particularly strong for colonial, Savannah, and coastal Georgia records. Research Center in Savannah.

Gateway to Georgia's digital collections including newspapers, photographs, government documents, and manuscript materials from institutions across the state. Includes Vanishing Georgia, Civil War collections, and county-level digitized records.

UGA's special collections. Extensive Georgia manuscript collections including family papers, plantation records, business records, and maps. Richard B. Russell Library for Political Research and Studies is also at UGA.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: county records (deeds, marriages, probate), vital records, cemetery records, military records, and church records. Extensive microfilm partnership with Georgia Archives provides digitized county records.
Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, burned county information, and links to digitized microfilm.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Georgia newspapers from the 1790s forward. Includes Augusta Chronicle (one of the South's oldest newspapers), Savannah papers, and smaller county weeklies.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Georgia Historic Newspapers (Digital Library of Georgia)
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized Georgia newspapers not in Chronicling America. Includes county weeklies that document legal notices, estates, marriage announcements, and community events across rural Georgia.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Tax digests listing enslaved persons by slaveholder (1790-1865), estate inventories naming enslaved individuals, bills of sale, and manumission records. Tax digests are often the best surviving records for burned counties. Also holds Freedmen's Bureau records indexes.

Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands for Georgia (1865-1872). Labor contracts, marriage registrations, school reports, hospital records, and complaint files. Many records indexed on FamilySearch. NARA microfilm M798, M1903.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Auburn Avenue Research Library on African American Culture and History
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Major research center for African American history in Georgia. Holdings include manuscripts, photographs, oral histories, and published materials focused on Atlanta and Georgia African American communities.
Born in Slavery: Slave Narratives from the Federal Writers' Project (1936-1938). Georgia has one of the largest collections of WPA slave narratives. Provides first-person accounts of enslaved life.

native_american_and_removal_records.description: Cherokee census rolls (1817-1835), property valuations, emigration records, and Treaty of New Echota (1835) documentation. Cherokee National Capital was at New Echota in Gordon County. Removal completed 1838 (Trail of Tears). NARA Record Group 75.

Creek Nation records including land cessions (Treaty of Indian Springs 1825, Treaty of Washington 1826), census rolls, and removal records. Creek lands in central and western Georgia were distributed through the land lottery system (1827). NARA Record Group 75.

Georgia distributed former Creek and Cherokee lands through a unique lottery system (1805, 1807, 1820, 1821, 1827, 1832, 1833). Lottery records name all eligible participants and winners. Essential for tracing settlement in central, western, and north Georgia counties.

military_records.description: Confederate pension applications (1879-1960), Confederate service records, militia records, Revolutionary War records, War of 1812 records, Indian War records, and WWI/WWII service cards. Confederate pension files are particularly valuable — often include family details, residence history, and unit information.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers & Sailors System — Georgia
military_records.description: Database of Confederate and Union soldiers from Georgia. Includes unit assignments and basic service information. Cross-reference with GA Archives Confederate pension files.

land_and_property.description: Unique to Georgia — land distributed by lottery rather than federal land office. Seven lotteries distributed former Native American lands. Records include: eligible persons lists, fortunate drawers, grants, and plats. Published indexes available.

Colonial-era headright grants (1755-1775) and state-era headright grants (1783-1803). Warrants and surveys document original land grants. Published in 'The Colonial Records of the State of Georgia' and other compilations.

vital_records.source_name: Georgia Department of Public Health — Vital Records
vital_records.repository: GA Dept of Public Health, Atlanta
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from 1919 (compliance improved ~1928). For earlier vital events, check county Probate Court marriage records, church registers, cemetery records, and city records (Atlanta, Savannah, Augusta kept earlier records). Historical birth/death records (100+ years) transferred to Georgia Archives.

church_and_cemetery.description: Microfilmed church records from congregations across Georgia. Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Episcopal records are most prevalent. Colonial-era records from Christ Church (Savannah), Midway Congregational Church (Liberty County), and Jerusalem Evangelical Lutheran Church (Effingham County — Salzburger settlement) are among Georgia's oldest.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Georgia. With 159 counties and many rural cemeteries, coverage varies significantly. Family cemeteries on former plantation lands are particularly important for African American genealogy.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes Georgia Genealogical Society Quarterly (since 1964). Maintains research resources and county-level research guides.

Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, obituary lookups, historical sketches, Bible records, and county-specific research guides for all 159 Georgia counties.
Authoritative reference for Georgia history, including county histories, biographical entries, and cultural topics. Useful for understanding county formation, local history, and historical context.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Georgia has two abolished counties: Campbell County (abolished 1932, merged into Fulton County) and Milton County (abolished 1932, merged into Fulton and part of north Fulton). Records for both counties are now maintained by Fulton County. Researchers with ancestors in northern metro Atlanta should check both Fulton County and the former Milton/Campbell county records at Georgia Archives. Additionally, the 1777 parish system preceded the county system — records from St. George, St. Paul, Christ Church, St. Philip, St. Matthew, St. Andrew, St. James, St. John, St. David, St. Patrick, St. Mary, and St. Thomas parishes are at Georgia Archives and the Georgia Historical Society.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Georgia has one of the highest rates of courthouse record loss in the United States. Causes include: Civil War destruction (Sherman's March to the Sea, 1864 — affected Cherokee, Cobb, Floyd, Fulton, Gordon, Henry, Murray, Paulding, Washington, and Whitfield counties), antebellum courthouse fires (1840s-1850s — affected Baker, Bulloch, Clay, Clinch, Columbia, Decatur, Early, Emanuel, Jasper, Laurens, Pike, Pulaski, Randolph, and others), and post-war courthouse fires. Paul K. Graham's research documents approximately 75 counties with significant record losses. Tax digests at the Georgia Archives often survive when other records are destroyed and serve as the primary substitute source.
